  A 10,000 ft grid distance at a 0.9999 scale factor and 5,280 ft ellipsoid height is 10,003.53 ft on the ground. h is
  the ellipsoid height (orthometric H + geoid N). Fuzzer pins the CF identity, the reverse, and linearity.
  calc-survey.js gzip cap 13500 -> 15500 B. Home count 1,224 -> 1,225.

### feat(machining): spec-v775 gear-tooth chordal thickness (caliper); 2026-07-14

- New tile `gear-chordal-thickness` (Group K, calc-machining.js): the gear-tooth caliper settings the geometry tile
  leaves out -- chordal tooth thickness `tc = (N/Pd) sin(90/N deg)` and chordal addendum
  `ac = 1/Pd + (N/2Pd)(1 - cos(90/N deg))` for a 20-degree full-depth involute spur gear. Pd 10, N 40 -> tc 0.1570 in,
  ac 0.1015 in (arc thickness pi/2Pd = 0.15708). Fuzzer pins the chord-under-arc relation, the 1/Pd scaling, and the
  large-N limit. calc-machining.js gzip cap 19500 -> 21000 B. Home count 1,223 -> 1,224.

### feat(trucking): spec-v774 low-speed off-tracking (swept path); 2026-07-14

- New tile `truck-off-tracking` (Group J, calc-trucking.js): how far a turning truck's rear axle tracks inside the front
  axle's path -- `OT = R - sqrt(R^2 - sum(L^2))`, R the turn radius and L each unit's wheelbase (tractor wheelbase +
  trailer kingpin-to-axle, summed in quadrature). A 20 ft wheelbase on a 50 ft radius off-tracks 4.2 ft. Fuzzer pins the
  closed form, the quadrature equivalence, and the tighter-radius/longer-wheelbase monotonicity. calc-trucking.js gzip
  cap 30000 -> 32000 B. Home count 1,222 -> 1,223.

### feat(agriculture): spec-v773 bunker (horizontal) silo forage capacity; 2026-07-14

- New tile `bunker-silo-capacity` (Group L, calc-agriculture.js): the forage a horizontal bunker or trench silo holds --
  trapezoidal cross-section `(bottom + top)/2 x average depth`, times length, times as-fed density, over 2,000 lb/ton. A
  30 ft wide, 8 ft deep, 100 ft bunker at 44 lb/ft^3 holds ~528 tons. As-fed density is a user input (corn silage
  ~40-50 lb/ft^3). Fuzzer pins the trapezoidal-prism volume and the linear scalings. calc-agriculture.js gzip cap
  54000 -> 56000 B. Home count 1,221 -> 1,222.

### feat(arborist): spec-v772 clinometer tree height (percent-slope); 2026-07-14

- New tile `tree-height-clinometer` (Group L, calc-arborist.js): tree height from a clinometer's percent scale --
  `H = D x (top% - base%)/100`, both readings signed (+ above eye level, - below). At 100 ft with a +58% top and a -4%
  base (below eye) the tree is 62 ft. When the base is below eye its height adds; uphill it subtracts. Fuzzer pins the
  closed form, the signed-base rule, and linearity in distance. calc-arborist.js gzip cap 17500 -> 19000 B.
  Home count 1,220 -> 1,221.

### feat(survey): spec-v771 leveling curvature-and-refraction correction; 2026-07-14

- New tile `leveling-curvature-refraction` (Group P, calc-survey.js): the correction the leveling and slope-reduction
  tiles say they skip -- `h_cr = 0.0206 K^2` ft (K = sight distance in thousands of feet), the curvature `0.0239 K^2`
  minus refraction `0.0033 K^2`, subtracted from the far rod. A 2,000 ft sight is 0.0824 ft; a 1-mile sight is 0.574 ft.
  Fuzzer pins the component split, the mile form, and the quadratic scaling. calc-survey.js gzip cap 12000 -> 13500 B.
  Home count 1,219 -> 1,220.

### feat(mechanic): spec-v770 helical compression spring rate; 2026-07-14

- New tile `helical-spring-rate` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js): the axial rate of a coil spring from its geometry --
  `k = G d^4 / (8 D^3 Na)`, with the wire shear modulus G by material (music wire, hard-drawn, chrome-silicon, stainless,
  phosphor bronze). A 0.080 in hard-drawn wire, 0.75 in mean coil, 8 active coils is 17.4 lb/in (spring index 9.4). Flags
  a spring index outside 4-12. Fuzzer pins the d^4 / 1/D^3 / 1/Na scaling laws. calc-mechanic.js gzip cap 44000 -> 46000 B.
  tools-data.js registry cap 185000 -> 200000 B (the eight forward tiles this batch grew the shared registry past the old
  cap). Home count 1,218 -> 1,219.

### feat(survey): spec-v769 total-station slope-to-horizontal reduction; 2026-07-14

- New tile `edm-slope-reduction` (Group P, calc-survey.js): the right-triangle reduction of an EDM/total-station slope
  distance -- zenith angle `H = S sinZ`, `V = S cosZ`; vertical angle `H = S cosa`, `V = S sina`; ground-to-ground
  elevation difference `= V + HI - HR`. A 250 ft slope shot at an 86 deg zenith is 249.39 ft horizontal and 17.44 ft up.
  The step that feeds the horizontal distance to `cogo-forward-point`. Angle-type select (zenith or vertical). Fuzzer
  pins H^2+V^2=S^2 and zenith/vertical equivalence. calc-survey.js gzip cap 10500 -> 12000 B. Home count 1,217 -> 1,218.

### feat(treatment): spec-v768 pool water volume by shape; 2026-07-14

- New tile `pool-volume` (Group M, calc-treatment.js): the gallonage every pool dose and heater tile takes as its input
  -- `gallons = surface area x average depth x 7.48052`, `average depth = (shallow + deep)/2`; rectangle `L x W`, round
  `pi(D/2)^2`, oval `(pi/4)L x W`. A 32 x 16 ft rectangle 3 to 8 ft deep is 512 ft^2 x 5.5 ft = 2,816 ft^3 = 21,065 gal.
  Shape select. calc-treatment.js gzip cap 26000 -> 27500 B. Home count 1,216 -> 1,217.

### feat(civil): spec-v767 curve deflection-angle stakeout; 2026-07-14

- New tile `curve-deflection-stakeout` (Group E, calc-civil.js): the deflection-angle method horizontal-curve leaves out
  -- an arc length `l` from the PC turns a deflection from the back tangent `delta = (l/2R)(180/pi)` and a sub-chord
  `c = 2R sin(l/2R)`; arc-definition `D = 5729.58/R`. On a 500 ft radius, 100 ft of arc is 5.7296 deg and a 99.83 ft
  chord. Enter radius or degree of curve. Fuzzer cross-checks horizontal-curve (full-arc deflection = half the central
  angle, full-arc chord = long chord). calc-civil.js gzip cap 12500 -> 14000 B. Home count 1,215 -> 1,216.

### feat(survey): spec-v766 COGO forward locate from bearing and distance; 2026-07-14

- New tile `cogo-forward-point` (Group P, calc-survey.js): the coordinate-geometry forward step -- from a known point, an
  azimuth (clockwise from north), and a distance, the new coordinates. `dN = D cos(Az)`, `dE = D sin(Az)`;
  `N2 = N1 + dN`, `E2 = E1 + dE`. N5000/E5000 at azimuth 45 deg, 200 ft lands N5141.42/E5141.42. The single forward
  step traverse-closure only ever sums over a whole loop. calc-survey.js gzip cap 9000 -> 10500 B. Home count 1,214 -> 1,215.

### feat(pipefit): spec-v765 boiler horsepower, steam output, and EDR (ABMA/ASME ratings); 2026-07-14

- New tile `boiler-horsepower` (Group B, calc-pipefit.js): the ABMA/ASME boiler ratings from a gross output --
  `BHP = output / 33,475`, `steam = BHP x 34.5` lb/hr (from and at 212 F), and `EDR = BHP x 139` sq ft. A 500,000 Btu/hr
  boiler is 14.94 BHP, 515 lb/hr, and 2,076 sq ft of radiation. First non-inverse tile after the inverse campaign closed.
  calc-pipefit.js gzip cap 21000 -> 22500 B. Home tile count 1,213 -> 1,214.

### feat(water): spec-v764 chlorine decay constant from a bottle test (chlorine-decay inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `chlorine-decay-constant` (Group M, calc-water.js): the first-order decay constant k from an initial and a
  measured residual over an elapsed time, `k = ln(C0 / C) / t`, with the half-life. A residual falling 2.0 -> 0.7358 mg/L
  in 10 h gives k = 0.100 1/hr (a 6.93 h half-life). Round-trips through `computeChlorineDecay` across a C0/k/t sweep.
  calc-water.js gzip cap 28500 -> 30000 B. Home tile count 1,212 -> 1,213.

### feat(edu): spec-v763 sample size for a target margin of error (confidence-interval inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `sample-size-for-margin` (Group Y, calc-edu.js): the sample size needed so a proportion Wald margin of error
  meets a target. `n = z^2 * p(1-p) / E^2`, rounded up. A +/- 3-point survey at 95% with the worst-case p = 0.5 needs
  1,068 responses. Inverts the proportion side of `confidence-interval`; the round-trip through `computeConfidenceInterval`
  confirms the achieved margin meets the target. calc-edu.js gzip cap 35000 -> 37000 B. Home tile count 1,211 -> 1,212.

### feat(lab): spec-v762 projected cell count from doubling time (doubling-time inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `growth-projected-count` (Group T, calc-lab.js): the projected count (or OD) from a known doubling time over an
  elapsed time. `N = N0 x 2^(t / Td)`. 100,000 cells at an 8 h doubling time reach 800,000 in 24 h (3 doublings, 8x).
  Round-trips through `doubling-time`. Closes Explore sweep #15. Home tile count 1,210 -> 1,211.

### feat(refrigerant): spec-v761 COP implied by the heat of rejection (condenser-heat-rejection inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `condenser-cop-for-heat-rejection` (Group C, calc-refrigerant.js): the COP implied by a measured/rated heat of
  rejection and the evaporator capacity. `COP = Q_evap / (THR - Q_evap)`. A system absorbing 60,000 Btu/h and rejecting
  100,000 runs a COP of 1.5. Round-trips through `condenser-heat-rejection`. Home tile count 1,209 -> 1,210.

### feat(metalair): spec-v760 groove weld length for an applied load (groove-weld-strength inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `groove-weld-length-for-load` (Group E, calc-metalair.js): the weld length an applied load needs at a given
  effective throat. `L = load / (stress_ksi x 1000 x throat)` (AISC J2, ASD 0.30 FEXX / LRFD 0.75 x 0.60 FEXX). A
  100,000 lb LRFD load on a 0.25 in E70 PJP throat needs ~12.7 in. Round-trips through `groove-weld-strength`. Home tile
  count 1,208 -> 1,209.

### feat(pipefit): spec-v759 steam PRV orifice area for a required capacity (steam-prv-napier inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `steam-prv-area-for-capacity` (Group B, calc-pipefit.js): the orifice / seat area a required steam relief
  capacity needs. `A = W / (51.43 Cd P1)` (choked Napier). A 5,000 lb/hr relief at 100 psia (Cd 0.9) wants ~1.08 in^2 --
  round up to an API 526 orifice letter. Round-trips through `steam-prv-napier`. Home tile count 1,207 -> 1,208.

### feat(cross): spec-v758 wind speed from wind chill and temperature (wind-chill inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `wind-chill-wind-speed` (Group G, calc-cross.js): the wind speed that produces a target/reported wind chill at
  a known air temperature. `w = [ (WC - 35.74 - 0.6215 T) / (0.4275 T - 35.75) ]^(1/0.16)` (NWS 2001). At 5 F air, a -19 F
  wind chill implies ~30 mph. Valid for T <= 50 F and w >= 3 mph. Round-trips through `wind-chill`. Home tile count 1,206
  -> 1,207.

### feat(septic): spec-v757 septic LPP squirt head for a target orifice flow (septic-lpp-orifice inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `septic-lpp-squirt-head` (Group B, calc-septic.js): the squirt head (residual pressure) that produces a target
  per-orifice discharge. `h = ( Q / (19.63 Cd d^2) )^2`. A 0.25 in orifice at Cd 0.6 delivering 1.28 gpm needs ~3.0 ft
  (1.3 psi); flags whether it lands in the LPP 2.5-5 ft band. Round-trips through `septic-lpp-orifice`. Home tile count
  1,205 -> 1,206.

### feat(civil): spec-v756 safe curve speed from radius and superelevation (superelevation speed inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `superelevation-safe-curve-speed` (Group E, calc-civil.js): the maximum safe speed a curve supports from its
  radius, superelevation, and side-friction factor. `V = sqrt( 15 R (e + f) )`. A 1,500 ft radius at e 0.08, f 0.12
  supports ~67 mph. The speed is always an input in the forward tile's modes; this solves the remaining variable.
  Round-trips through `superelevation`. Opens Explore sweep #15. Home tile count 1,204 -> 1,205.
- citations.js gzip cap 410000 -> 440000 B: the 30 inverse-tile citation blocks added this session (v727-v756) crossed
  the old cap (100.1%). Lazy-loaded (prerendered into shells at build), not in the home-view payload.

### feat(gas): spec-v755 gas leak equivalent hole diameter (gas-leak-rate inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `gas-leak-hole-diameter` (Group B, calc-gas.js): the equivalent orifice (hole) diameter from a measured gas
  leak rate. `d = sqrt( 4 Q / (3550 c pi sqrt(dP / SG)) )`. A 3.15 cfh natural-gas leak at 0.25 psi (c 0.7) is ~0.050 in.
  An estimate of the effective hole, not a code leak-test method. Round-trips through `gas-leak-rate`. Closes Explore
  sweep #14. Home tile count 1,203 -> 1,204.

### feat(steel): spec-v754 required moment of inertia for a deflection limit (steel-camber inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `steel-inertia-for-deflection` (Group E, calc-steel.js): the moment of inertia a simple-span beam needs to
  hold the uniform-load midspan deflection to a limit. `I = 5 w L^4 / (384 E delta_allow)`. A 1.0 kip/ft, 40 ft beam held
  to 1.0 in needs Ix ~1,986 in^4 (span/360 = span x 12 / 360). Round-trips through `steel-camber`. Home tile count 1,202
  -> 1,203.

### feat(concrete): spec-v753 RC column longitudinal steel for a target load (rc-column-axial inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `rc-column-steel-for-load` (Group E, calc-concrete.js): the longitudinal steel a target factored axial load
  needs for a given tied column. `Ast = (phi Pn / 0.52 - 0.85 f'c Ag) / (fy - 0.85 f'c)`. A 16 in square 4,000 psi column
  at 639 kip needs ~6.33 in^2 (2.47%). Reports max(strength, 1% min) and flags loads over 8% (section too small).
  Round-trips through `rc-column-axial`. Home tile count 1,201 -> 1,202.

### feat(concrete): spec-v752 concrete section depth for a target cracking moment (concrete-cracking-moment inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `concrete-depth-for-cracking-moment` (Group E, calc-concrete.js): the total section depth that reaches a target
  cracking moment for a given width. `h = sqrt( 6 Mcr / (fr b) )`, `fr = 7.5 lambda sqrt(f'c)`. A 12 in wide 4000 psi
  section needs ~20 in to crack at 31.6 kip-ft (enter 1.2 Mcr for the minimum-reinforcement check). Round-trips through
  `concrete-cracking-moment`. Home tile count 1,200 -> 1,201.

### feat(layout): spec-v751 arc rise (sagitta) from radius and chord (circular-arc inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `circular-arc-rise-from-radius` (Group G, calc-layout.js): the rise (sagitta / middle ordinate) of an arc from
  a known radius and chord. `rise = R - sqrt(R^2 - (chord/2)^2)`. A 24 in chord on a 20 in radius rises 4.0 in at midspan;
  also reports the arc length and central angle. Round-trips through `circular-arc`. **Catalog's 1,200th tile.** Home tile
  count 1,199 -> 1,200.

### feat(elecdesign): spec-v750 luminaire mounting height for a target illuminance (point-illuminance inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `luminaire-height-for-illuminance` (Group A, calc-elecdesign.js): the mounting height that lands a target
  horizontal illuminance at a point. `h = sqrt( I x cos^3(angle) / E )`. A 1,000 cd source needs a 10 ft mount for 10 fc
  directly below. Solves for height (distinct from `point-method-required-candela`, which solves for the intensity).
  Round-trips through `point-illuminance`. Home tile count 1,198 -> 1,199.

### feat(geotech): spec-v749 infinite slope critical depth for a target FS (slope-stability-infinite inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `slope-failure-depth-for-fs` (Group E, calc-geotech.js): the critical failure-plane depth at which the
  infinite-slope FS drops to a target. `H = c' / (gamma cos beta (FS sin beta - cos beta tan phi'))`. A 25 deg cut in
  c' 200 psf, phi' 30, gamma 120 soil reaches FS 1.5 at ~16.6 ft. Guards c' > 0 and a target above the deep limit
  tan phi'/tan beta. Round-trips through `slope-stability-infinite`. Home tile count 1,197 -> 1,198.

### feat(geotech): spec-v748 pile group spacing for a target efficiency (pile-group-efficiency inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `pile-group-spacing-for-efficiency` (Group E, calc-geotech.js): the center-to-center spacing that reaches a
  target Converse-Labarre efficiency. `theta = (1-Eg) x 90 m n / ((n-1)m+(m-1)n)`, then `s = d / tan(theta)`. A 3x3 group
  of 12 in piles at Eg 0.75 needs ~39.6 in (3.3d). Round-trips through `pile-group-efficiency`. Home tile count 1,196 ->
  1,197.

### feat(construction): spec-v747 solid shaft diameter for an allowable torsion (shaft-torsion inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `shaft-diameter-for-torsion` (Group E, calc-construction.js): the minimum solid-shaft diameter that keeps the
  torsional shear stress within an allowable. `d = (16 T / (pi tau_allow))^(1/3)`. A 12,000 lb-in torque at an 8,000 psi
  allowable needs ~1.97 in. Reports the angle of twist with a length and G. Round-trips through `shaft-torsion`. Opens
  Explore sweep #14. Home tile count 1,195 -> 1,196.

### feat(hvac): spec-v746 pipe insulation thickness for a target heat loss (pipe-heat-loss-radial inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `insulation-thickness-for-heat-loss` (Group C, calc-hvac.js): the insulation thickness that caps radial pipe
  heat loss at a target rate. `thickness = (od/2) x (exp(2*pi*(k/12)*(hot-amb)/q) - 1)`. A 2 in pipe at 200 F in 70 F air
  with k 0.25 held to 40 BTU/hr/ft needs ~0.53 in. Targets a heat-loss budget (distinct from the surface-temperature
  `insulation-thickness` tile). Round-trips through `pipe-heat-loss-radial`. Closes Explore sweep #13. Home tile count
  1,194 -> 1,195.

### feat(plumbing): spec-v745 max line pressure for a thrust block (thrust-block-sizing inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `thrust-block-max-pressure` (Group B, calc-plumbing.js): the highest test/surge pressure a thrust block of a
  given bearing-face area restrains at a bend. `P = Ab x soil / (2 A sin(theta/2))`. A 4.13 ft^2 block on an 8 in main at
  a 90-degree bend in 2,000 psf soil holds ~100 psi. Round-trips through `thrust-block-sizing`. Home tile count 1,193 ->
  1,194.

### feat(rigging): spec-v744 spreader beam minimum top-point height (spreader-beam inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `spreader-beam-min-height` (Group Z, calc-rigging.js): the minimum top-point height so the top-sling tension
  stays within the sling WLL. `angle = asin( load / (2 x WLL) )`, then `top = (bar/2) x tan(angle)`. A 10,000 lb load on
  a 10 ft bar with 6,000 lb slings needs at least 7.54 ft. Round-trips through `spreader-beam`. Home tile count 1,192 ->
  1,193.

### feat(solar): spec-v743 PV max ambient temperature for a target power (pv-cell-temperature-power inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `pv-max-ambient-for-power` (Group A, calc-solar.js): the highest ambient temperature a module still makes a
  target power. `T_cell = 25 + (P/P_stc - 1) x 100/gamma`, then `T_amb = T_cell - (NOCT-20) x G/800`. A 400 W module
  holding 358 W at 800 W/m^2 tops out at 30 C air (55 C cell). Round-trips through `pv-cell-temperature-power`. Home tile
  count 1,191 -> 1,192.

### feat(treatment): spec-v742 clarifier surface area for a target SOR (clarifier-surface-loading inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `clarifier-area-for-loading` (Group M, calc-treatment.js): the clarifier surface area a target surface
  overflow rate needs at the design flow. `area = flow x 1e6 / SOR`, plus the equivalent circular diameter. 1 MGD at 800
  gpd/ft^2 needs 1,250 ft^2 (~40 ft diameter). Round-trips through `clarifier-surface-loading`. Home tile count 1,190 ->
  1,191.

### feat(lowvoltage): spec-v741 camera max distance for a pixel density (camera-lens-fov inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `camera-max-distance-for-ppf` (Group A, calc-lowvoltage.js): the farthest distance a camera still meets a
  target pixel density (a DORI task). `distance = px x focal / (target ppf x sensor)`. A 1920 px / 4 mm / 5.37 mm camera
  holds 76 ppf (Identify) out to ~18.8 ft. Round-trips through `camera-lens-fov`. Home tile count 1,189 -> 1,190.

### feat(lowvoltage): spec-v740 ceiling speaker coverage angle for a target spacing (ceiling-speaker-coverage inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `ceiling-speaker-coverage-angle` (Group A, calc-lowvoltage.js): the coverage angle a target coverage diameter
  (or on-center spacing) needs at a mounting drop. `angle = 2 x atan( diameter / (2 x (ceiling - ear)) )`. An 8 ft circle
  under a 10 ft ceiling over 4 ft ears needs a 67 deg speaker. Round-trips through `ceiling-speaker-coverage`. Home tile
  count 1,188 -> 1,189.

### feat(rigging): spec-v739 max redirect angle for a block WLL (block-redirect-load inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `block-redirect-max-angle` (Group Z, calc-rigging.js): the largest direction change a block or anchor of a
  rated WLL can turn a line without the resultant exceeding the rating. `angle = 2 x asin( WLL / (2 x line tension) )`,
  capped at 180 deg when WLL >= 2T. A 3,000 lb line through a 5,000 lb block can turn up to 112.9 deg. Round-trips through
  `block-redirect-load`. Home tile count 1,187 -> 1,188.

### feat(fire): spec-v738 smooth-bore tip diameter for a target flow (smooth-bore-flow inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `smooth-bore-diameter-for-flow` (Group F, calc-fire.js): the tip (bore) diameter a target flow needs at a
  nozzle pressure. `d = sqrt( gpm / (29.7 x sqrt(NP)) )`. 250 gpm at 50 psi wants a 1.09 in tip (~93 lb reaction).
  Round-trips through `smooth-bore-flow`. Opens Explore sweep #13. Home tile count 1,186 -> 1,187.

### feat(fab): spec-v737 wire feed speed for a target deposition rate (wire-feed-deposition inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `wire-feed-speed-for-deposition` (Group E, calc-fab.js): the wire feed speed a target deposition rate needs.
  `WFS = deposit / (60 x (pi/4 x dia^2) x 0.2836 x eff)`. A 6 lb/hr target on 0.035 in wire at 92% needs ~398 in/min.
  Round-trips through `wire-feed-deposition`. Home tile count 1,185 -> 1,186.

### feat(agriculture): spec-v736 sprinkler zone flow for a target precip rate (sprinkler-precip-rate inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `sprinkler-gpm-for-precip` (Group L, calc-agriculture.js): the total zone flow a target precipitation rate
  needs over a covered area. `gpm = PR x area / 96.3`. A 1.5 in/hr rate over 1,200 ft^2 needs ~18.7 gpm of heads.
  Round-trips through `sprinkler-precip-rate`. Home tile count 1,184 -> 1,185.

### feat(motor): spec-v735 motor run hours for an energy budget (motor-operating-cost inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `motor-run-hours-for-budget` (Group A, calc-motor.js): the run hours an annual energy budget buys.
  `hours = budget / (input_kW x rate)`, `input_kW = HP x 0.746 x load / efficiency`. A 25 HP motor at 93% and $0.12/kWh
  draws 20.05 kW, so a $5,000 budget covers ~2,078 hours. Round-trips through `motor-operating-cost`. Home tile count
  1,183 -> 1,184.

### feat(water): spec-v734 filter area for a target loading rate (filter-loading inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `filter-area-for-loading` (Group M, calc-water.js): the filter area needed for a target loading rate at the
  design flow. `area = flow / target_loading`. 800 GPM at 4 gpm/ft^2 needs 200 ft^2 (3,000 GPM backwash). Reports the
  backwash flow and names the loading band. Round-trips through `filter-loading`. Home tile count 1,182 -> 1,183.

### feat(machining): spec-v733 countersink diameter from a plunge depth (countersink-depth inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `countersink-diameter-from-depth` (Group K, calc-machining.js): the finished countersink diameter a set plunge
  depth opens. `D_cs = 2 Z tan(angle/2) + d_hole`. A 0.1438 in plunge with an 82 deg tool and a 0.250 in pilot opens a
  0.500 in countersink. Round-trips through `countersink-depth`. Home tile count 1,181 -> 1,182.

### feat(machining): spec-v732 cutter diameter for a spindle RPM (cutting-speed-rpm inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `cutting-diameter-for-rpm` (Group K, calc-machining.js): the cutter or work diameter that runs at a target (or
  maximum) spindle RPM for a given surface speed. `diameter = 12 x SFM / (pi x RPM)`. 100 SFM at 1,000 RPM is a 0.382 in
  diameter. A machine RPM ceiling sets the smallest cutter that still reaches the full SFM. Round-trips through
  `cutting-speed-rpm`. Home tile count 1,180 -> 1,181.

### feat(drainage): spec-v731 scupper width for a required overflow flow (overflow-scupper-sizing inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `scupper-width-for-flow` (Group B, calc-drainage.js): the scupper width for a required overflow flow at a
  design head, from the Francis rectangular weir. `L = Q / (3.33 H^1.5)` suppressed, `+ 0.2 H` contracted. 118 gpm at a
  3.5 in head needs a 6.0 in opening (6.7 in contracted). Round-trips through `overflow-scupper-sizing` (both forms).
  Home tile count 1,179 -> 1,180.

### feat(arborist): spec-v730 minimum sound shell for an allowable trunk strength loss (trunk-decay-strength inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `trunk-min-shell-thickness` (Group L, calc-arborist.js): the minimum radial sound-wood shell thickness for a
  maximum acceptable strength loss. `t = (D/2) x (1 - (loss/100)^(1/3))`. A 24-in trunk held to a 29.6% loss needs a
  4.0-in shell (t/R 0.33). Flags a shell below the Mattheck t/R 0.30 concern. Round-trips through `trunk-decay-strength`.
  Home tile count 1,178 -> 1,179.

### feat(machining): spec-v729 drill point angle from tip length (drill-point-depth inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `drill-point-angle-from-length` (Group K, calc-machining.js): the included point angle from the diameter and a
  measured or target tip length. `angle = 2 x atan( (diameter/2) / point_length )`. A 0.5 in drill with a 0.15 in tip is a
  118-degree point. Round-trips through `drill-point-depth`. Opens Explore sweep #12. Home tile count 1,177 -> 1,178.

### feat(shop): spec-v728 interference for a target press-fit holding force (press-fit-pressure inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `press-fit-interference-for-force` (Group G, calc-shop.js): the diametral interference that reaches a target
  axial holding force. `i = holding x 2 Do^2 / (E x (Do^2 - D^2) x pi x D x L x mu)` (linear in interference; the
  interface diameter D cancels). Reports the resulting contact pressure and hub bore stress, and flags a burst against an
  optional hub yield. A 25,447 lb target on a 2 in shaft / 4 in steel hub wants 0.0020 in. Round-trips through
  `press-fit-pressure`. Closes the sweep-11 inverse queue. Home tile count 1,176 -> 1,177.

### feat(stage): spec-v727 max screen size for a projector (projector-brightness inverse); 2026-07-14

- New tile `projector-max-screen-size` (Group N, calc-stage.js): the largest screen a projector lights to a target
  brightness. `max_area = lumens x gain / target_fL`, then width / height / diagonal at the aspect ratio. A 5,000-lumen
  projector on a unity-gain screen at 16 fL tops out near a 27 ft (16:9) diagonal. Round-trips through
  `projector-brightness`. Home tile count 1,175 -> 1,176.

### feat(mechanic): spec-v726 max boost before a charge-air temperature limit (turbo-pressure-ratio inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `turbo-max-boost-for-charge-temp` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the inverse of `turbo-pressure-ratio`. The forward tile gives the charge-air (compressor-outlet) temperature from a boost; the tuning question is the reverse: how much boost before the charge-air temperature hits a limit. From `T_out = T_in x [1 + (PR^0.283 - 1)/eff]`, the inverse is `PR = [1 + eff x (T_out/T_in - 1)]^(1/0.283)` and `boost = ambient x (PR - 1)` (temperatures absolute). The pinned example: an 80 F inlet, 70% compressor, 250 F charge-air limit tops out near **15 psi**; a more efficient 78% compressor allows ~17 psi. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a limit/inlet/efficiency sweep by feeding the max boost back through `computeTurboPressureRatio` and confirming the compressor-outlet temp equals the limit. A limit at or below the inlet is guarded (compressing air only heats it). Full wiring: `tools-data` (beside turbo-pressure-ratio, past the Group K exact-12 audit block), `tile-meta`, `citations` (model solved for the boost, GOVERNANCE.general), compute-map, 1 machine-verified worked example, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles, the `app.js` mechanic declare, the `_simpleRenderer` factory renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(mechanic): spec-v725 waterline length for a target hull speed (hull-speed inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `waterline-for-hull-speed` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the inverse of `hull-speed`. The forward tile gives the displacement hull speed from a waterline length; the design question is the reverse: the waterline a hull needs to reach a target speed. From `hull_speed = 1.34 x sqrt(LWL)`, the inverse is `LWL = (target_speed / 1.34)^2`. The pinned example: an 8 kn displacement cruiser needs a **~35.6 ft** waterline; 12 kn needs ~80 ft (the quadratic penalty of chasing displacement speed). This is the displacement ceiling, not a hard limit -- a planing hull breaks free with enough power. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the waterline back through `computeHullSpeed` and confirming the hull speed equals the target. Full wiring: `tools-data` (beside hull-speed, past the Group K exact-12 audit block), `tile-meta`, `citations` (relation solved for the waterline, GOVERNANCE.general, Froude speed-length theory), compute-map, 1 machine-verified worked example, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles, the `app.js` mechanic declare, the `_simpleRenderer` factory renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(shop): spec-v724 press-brake max bendable thickness (press-brake-tonnage inverse); 2026-07-14

The forward tile gives the tonnage a bend needs; the everyday shop question is the reverse: the thickest material a given press can air-bend. From `total_tons = 575 x (UTS/60) x T^2 / V x L`, solving for T gives `T = sqrt(total_tons x V / (575 x (UTS/60) x L))`. The pinned example: a 100-ton press with a 0.5 in V-die over a 4 ft bend in mild steel tops out at **~0.147 in**; stronger stainless drops to ~0.120 in. From `concentrate = area x rate x (pct/100) x duration`, the inverse is `max_area = concentrate / (rate x (pct/100) x duration)`. ### feat(electrical): spec-v722 max motor HP for a starting-current budget (motor-locked-rotor-kva inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `motor-max-hp-for-starting-current` (Group A, calc-motor.js), the inverse of `motor-locked-rotor-kva`. The forward tile gives the locked-rotor (starting) current from a horsepower; the sizing question is the reverse: the largest motor a starting-current budget can start (a breaker, a generator's starting kVA, an SCCR / voltage-dip ceiling). Since LRA is linear in hp for a given code letter and voltage, `max_hp = budget / (kVA/hp x 1000 / (sqrt(3) x V))`, reusing the forward at hp=1 to carry the NEC Table 430.7(B) band and the 1-/3-phase relation. The pinned example: a 300 A budget on a code-G 460 V feeder tops out at **~38 hp**. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a current/code/phase sweep by feeding the max hp back through `computeMotorLockedRotorKva` and confirming the LRA equals the budget. ### feat(shop): spec-v721 pitch diameter from three-wire measurement (thread-measure-wire inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `thread-pitch-dia-from-wires` (Group G, calc-shop.js), the inverse of `thread-measure-wire` and the first tile of a fresh sweep-11 inverse queue. The forward tile gives the measurement over three wires M from a pitch diameter E, but that is backwards from the bench: the machinist measures M on the mic and wants E. Solving `M = E + 3W - 1.51553 P` for E gives `E = M - 3W + 1.51553 P`. The pinned example: a 1/2-13 thread read at M = 0.4900 in over best wires gives **E = 0.4734 in**, compared to the thread-class limits for the fit. Reuses the sibling's 60-degree geometry, best-wire default, range check, and inch/metric selector. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a TPI/measurement sweep by feeding E back through `computeThreadMeasureWire` and confirming the measurement equals M. ### feat(construction): spec-v720 basic wind speed from velocity pressure (wind-pressure inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `wind-speed-from-velocity-pressure` (Group E, calc-construction.js), the inverse of `wind-pressure`'s base velocity pressure and the tile that closes the sweep-10 inverse queue. The forward tile gives `q = 0.00256 V^2` (before Kz/Kzt/Kd/G/Cp); the check question is the reverse: the equivalent basic wind speed behind a bare velocity pressure. So `V = sqrt(q / 0.00256)`. The pinned example: a 25-psf velocity pressure corresponds to a **~98.8 mph** basic wind speed. To keep it unambiguous the tile inverts the velocity pressure only (not a Cp-loaded design surface pressure), and the note is explicit that a design surface pressure must first have Kz/Kzt/Kd/G/Cp divided out to recover q. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a velocity-pressure sweep by feeding the wind speed back through `computeWindPressure` and confirming its base q equals the input. ### feat(rigging): spec-v719 max wind speed before a load's swing limit (wind-on-load inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `max-wind-speed-for-lift` (Group Z, calc-rigging.js), the inverse of `wind-on-load`. The forward tile gives the swing angle from a wind speed; the in-service shutdown question is the reverse: the wind speed at which a suspended load reaches a maximum allowable swing. From `swing = atan(wind_lb / weight)` and `wind_lb = 0.00256 V^2 x area x shape`, the inverse is `V = sqrt(weight x tan(swing) / (0.00256 x area x shape))`. The pinned example: a 4,000-lb load with 200 ft^2 of sail reaches a 5-degree swing at **~20.7 mph**; a lighter load hits it sooner. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a swing/weight/area sweep by feeding the max wind speed back through `computeWindOnLoad` and confirming the swing equals the target. ### feat(electrical): spec-v717 max PF capacitor bank to keep resonance off a harmonic (harmonic-resonance inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `capacitor-bank-for-resonance-order` (Group A, calc-powerquality.js), the inverse of `harmonic-resonance`. The forward tile gives the parallel-resonance order from a bank size; the design question is the reverse: the largest PF capacitor bank that keeps the resonant order off the low harmonics. Since a bigger bank lowers the order, `h = sqrt(MVA_sc / MVAR_cap)` solved for the bank gives the maximum: `MVAR_cap = MVA_sc / h_target^2`. The pinned example: a 200-MVA bus targeting an order of 4.7 (to stay below the 5th) caps the bank at **~9.05 MVAR** -- a bigger bank walks the resonance down onto the 5th. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across an MVA/order sweep by feeding the max bank back through `computeHarmonicResonance` and confirming the resonant order equals the target. ### feat(plumbing): spec-v715 grease interceptor flow capacity (grease-trap inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `grease-interceptor-flow-capacity` (Group B, calc-plumbing.js), the inverse of `grease-trap`. The forward tile sizes the interceptor volume from a peak flow; the service question is the reverse: given an in-place interceptor, what peak flow is it rated to serve. From `volume = peak_flow x retention x loading`, the inverse is `peak_flow = volume / (retention x loading)`. The pinned example: a 1,000-gal interceptor at 30 min retention and 1.25 loading serves **~26.7 gpm** -- compare to the connected sinks' DFU flow to catch an undersized unit. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a volume/retention/loading sweep by feeding the served flow back through `computeGreaseTrap` and confirming the required volume equals the interceptor volume. ### feat(electrical): spec-v714 max grounding-grid resistance for the GPR screen (ground-potential-rise inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `max-grid-resistance-for-touch` (Group A, calc-elecdesign.js), the inverse of `ground-potential-rise`. The forward tile checks whether GPR = I_G x R_g clears the tolerable touch voltage; the design question is the reverse: what grid resistance target keeps the yard within the touch limit. From `GPR = grid_current x grid_resistance` and the screen passing when GPR <= tolerable_touch, the inverse is `max_grid_resistance = tolerable_touch / grid_current`. The pinned example: a 200-V touch limit with a 200-A grid current needs a grid resistance of **1.0 ohm** or less to clear the IEEE 80 screen without a full mesh/step study. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a touch/current sweep by feeding the max resistance back through `computeGroundPotentialRise` and confirming the GPR equals the touch limit and the screen passes at the limit. ### feat(rigging): spec-v713 wire-rope diameter for a required WLL (wire-rope-strength inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `wire-rope-diameter-for-wll` (Group Z, calc-rigging.js), the inverse of `wire-rope-strength` and the first tile of a fresh sweep-10 inverse queue. The forward tile gives the WLL from a diameter; the rigging question is the reverse: what diameter carries a required WLL. From `WLL = (construction_factor x d^2) / design_factor`, the inverse is `d = sqrt(WLL x design_factor / construction_factor)`, then rounded up to the next standard rope diameter (with its actual WLL reported). ### feat(geotech): spec-v712 required SPT N60 for a target bearing (spt-bearing-capacity inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `spt-required-n60` (Group E, calc-geotech.js), the inverse of `spt-bearing-capacity` and the tile that closes the sweep-9 inverse queue. The forward tile gives the allowable bearing from a blow count; the QA question is the reverse: what N60 must the boring show to carry a design pressure. Because qa is linear in N60 for both footing branches and Kd is independent of N60, `N60 = qa_target / qa(N60=1)`, reusing the forward at N60=1 to carry the branch and depth factor. The pinned example: a 6 ft footing 2 ft deep needing 5 ksf wants **N60 ~20** (round up for design). This is a settlement-controlled (serviceability) check, not the ultimate bearing capacity. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across a target/width/depth sweep (both branches) by feeding the required N60 back through `computeSptBearingCapacity` and confirming the allowable equals the target. ### feat(machining): spec-v711 max material removal rate from spindle power (spindle-power-torque inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `spindle-max-mrr` (Group K, calc-machining.js), the inverse of `spindle-power-torque`. The forward tile gives the motor horsepower a cut needs; the shop question is the reverse: given the motor, how much stock can it remove per minute. Since the spindle delivers `cutting_hp = motor_hp x efficiency` and each in3/min costs the unit power, `max_MRR = motor_hp x efficiency / unit_power`. The pinned example: a 5 hp spindle at 80% efficiency removes up to **4.0 in3/min** of carbon steel (unit power 1.0), or 12.1 in3/min of aluminum (0.33). This is the power (stall) limit only -- depth/feed, tool strength, and rigidity are separate limits. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse across an hp/unit-power/efficiency sweep by feeding the max MRR back through `computeSpindlePowerTorque` and confirming the motor hp equals the available motor. The forward tile gives the pitch from a design profile angle; on a constrained roof or lot the pitch is fixed, so the question is the reverse: what is the lowest sun elevation the array stays shade-free to. From `pitch = L cos(tilt) + L sin(tilt) / tan(prof)`, solving for the profile angle gives `prof = atan(L sin(tilt) / (pitch - L cos(tilt)))`, valid when the pitch exceeds the module footprint. ### feat(refrigerant): spec-v698 product pull-down time (product-pull-down-load inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `product-pull-down-time` (Group C, calc-refrigerant.js), the inverse of `product-pull-down-load`. The forward tile returns the rate from a chosen pull-down time (`rate = Q / hours`); the sizing question is the reverse: given the refrigeration capacity available for product load, how long does the pull-down take. So `hours = Q / capacity`, with Q (including the freezer sensible + latent-of-fusion + frozen-sensible branch) reused from `computeProductPullDownLoad` at 1 hour. The pinned example: 2,000 lb of produce (cp 0.9, 80 -> 35 F, Q = 81,000 Btu) at 3,375 Btu/hr pulls down in **24 hours** -- the forward design case read backward; a freezer load to 0 F (Q = 358,800 Btu) at the same capacity balloons to 106 hours, the signal to add capacity. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the hours back through `computeProductPullDownLoad` and confirming the rate equals the capacity. Full wiring: `tools-data` (beside product-pull-down-load, past every exact-count audit range), `tile-meta`, `citations` (ASHRAE Refrigeration relation solved for time, GOVERNANCE.general matching the sibling), compute-map, 1 machine-verified worked example, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (-> product-pull-down-load / walk-in-cooler-load / evaporator-td-dtd / refrigeration-cop), the `app.js` refrigerant declare, the hand-written renderer (capacity field in place of the time field), the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(hvac): spec-v697 max appliance input from room volume (combustion-air inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `combustion-air-max-input` (Group C, calc-hvac.js), the inverse of `combustion-air`. The forward tile sizes combustion-air openings from an appliance input and room volume; the install question is the reverse: given the mechanical closet, how big an appliance fits before combustion-air openings are required. IFGC 2021 §304.5 treats a space as adequate at 50 ft^3 per 1000 BTU/hr, so `max_input = (room_volume_ft3 / 50) x 1000`. The pinned example: a 4,000 ft^3 space supports **80,000 BTU/hr** by volume; a 10,000 ft^3 space supports 200,000 BTU/hr. The bounds-fuzzer proves the boundary by feeding the max input back through `computeCombustionAir` and confirming the required volume equals the room and `adequate_by_volume` is true. Full wiring: `tools-data` (beside combustion-air, un-audited Group C before the Group D marker), `tile-meta`, `citations` (IFGC §304 volume method solved for input, GOVERNANCE.mechanical matching the sibling), compute-map, 1 machine-verified worked example, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (-> combustion-air / excess-air-o2 / outdoor-air-ventilation), the `app.js` hvac declare, the hand-written single-input renderer (+ render-sentinel and dims annotation), regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. Verified locally with the CI-only shell gates. Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,145 -> 1,146 (version 0.287.0).

### feat(lowvoltage): spec-v696 CCTV retention days from disk capacity (cctv-storage inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `cctv-retention-days` (Group A, calc-lowvoltage.js), the inverse of `cctv-storage` (spec-v28). The forward tile sizes the disk for a retention target; the install question is the reverse: given the disk in the recorder, how many days does it hold. From `total_storage_GB = cameras x bitrate x 0.45 x hours x days`, `days = disk_GB / (cameras x bitrate x 0.45 x hours)`. The compute calls `computeCctvStorage` at `retention_days = 1` to reuse the daily-rate geometry. The pinned example: a 16 TB NVR with 8 cameras at 4 Mbps continuous holds **46 days**; switching to motion (50% duty) doubles it to **93 days**. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the retention back through `computeCctvStorage` and confirming it returns the disk capacity. Full wiring: `tools-data` (beside cctv-storage, past every exact-count audit range), `tile-meta`, `citations` (NVR storage relation solved for days, GOVERNANCE.electrical matching the sibling), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (-> cctv-storage / camera-lens-fov / poe-budget / structured-cabling-channel), the `app.js` lowvoltage declare, the hand-written renderer with the shared recording-mode select, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(trucking): spec-v695 max design speed from sight distance (stopping-sight-distance inverse); 2026-07-14

Lands `ssd-design-speed` (Group J, calc-trucking.js), the inverse of `stopping-sight-distance` (spec-v). The forward tile returns the required stopping sight distance from a speed; the roadway design question is the reverse: given the sight distance available to a crest or intersection, what is the fastest safe design speed. The AASHTO relation `SSD = 1.47 t v + v^2 / (30 (f + g))` is a quadratic in `v`, solved by the positive root `v = (-b + sqrt(b^2 + 4 a SSD)) / (2 a)` with `a = 1/(30(f+g))`, `b = 1.47 t`. The pinned example: 490 ft of sight distance on dry level pavement (f 0.35, t 2.5 s) yields **55 mph**; a -6% downgrade drops it to **51 mph** as the longer stop lowers the safe speed. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the design speed back through `computeStoppingSightDistance` and confirming it returns the sight distance. Full wiring: `tools-data` (placed in the later Group J section, NOT the exact-19 audit block beside stopping-sight-distance), `tile-meta`, `citations` (AASHTO Green Book, GOVERNANCE.trucking matching the sibling), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> stopping-sight-distance / braking-distance / vertical-curve-sight-distance), the `app.js` trucking declare, the hand-written renderer with the shared pavement-condition select, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. Verified locally with the CI-only shell gates. Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,143 -> 1,144 (version 0.285.0).

### feat(water): spec-v694 well sustainable yield from specific capacity (well-drawdown inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `well-max-yield` (Group M, calc-water.js), the inverse of `well-drawdown` (spec-v16). The forward tile returns the drawdown and specific capacity from a discharge; the pump-sizing question is the reverse: how hard can I pump this well without pulling the water below the pump. From `specific_capacity = discharge / drawdown`, `max_yield = specific_capacity x allowable_drawdown`. The pinned example: a 1.0 GPM/ft well with 30 ft of usable drawdown yields **30 GPM**; a marginal 0.3 GPM/ft well with 40 ft yields **12 GPM** (flagged marginal, below 0.5 GPM/ft). The note is explicit that specific capacity declines with rate, so the linear estimate holds near the tested rate and overstates the yield far above it. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the max yield back through `computeWellDrawdown` and confirming it returns the specific capacity. Full wiring: `tools-data` (placed in the later Group M section, NOT the exact-34 audit block beside well-drawdown), `tile-meta`, `citations` (AWWA A100 / USGS, GOVERNANCE.water + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> well-drawdown / pump-tdh / pump-eff-w2w / detention-time), the `app.js` water declare, the hand-written renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(electrical): spec-v693 fiber max length for a loss budget (fiber-loss-budget inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `fiber-max-length` (Group A, calc-lowvoltage.js), the inverse of `fiber-loss-budget`. The forward tile returns the total loss and margin for a run length; the install question is the reverse: how far can I run this fiber and still pass the loss budget. From `total_loss = (len/1000) x attenuation + connectors + splices`, `len_max = 1000 x (max_channel_loss - connector_loss - splice_loss) / attenuation`. The pinned example: a 2.6 dB budget on OM4 (3.0 dB/km) with two connectors reaches **367 m**; single-mode (0.4 dB/km) reaches **2,750 m**. The connector and splice losses are subtracted first; if they alone meet the budget, no length is available (rejected). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the max length back through `computeFiberLossBudget` and confirming the total loss equals the budget (margin 0). Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (TIA-568 / IEEE 802.3, GOVERNANCE.electrical + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> fiber-loss-budget / poe-budget / structured-cabling-channel / coax-rg-loss), the `app.js` lowvoltage declare, the hand-written renderer with the fiber/wavelength makeSelect, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,141 -> 1,142 (version 0.283.0).

### feat(mechanic): spec-v692 driveshaft max length for an operating speed (driveshaft-crit inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `driveshaft-max-length` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the inverse of `driveshaft-crit`. The forward tile returns the first-mode critical speed from a tube length; the design question is the reverse: how long can I make this tube before it whips at my operating speed. Because the critical speed falls as `1/length^2`, `L_max = L_ref x sqrt(0.65 x N_crit_ref / target_rpm)`, keeping the running speed at or below 0.65 of critical. The pinned example: a 3.5 in x 0.083 in steel tube at its 3,040.7 RPM safe limit maxes at **48 in**; halve the RPM and it grows 41% to **67.9 in** (sqrt(2)). It reuses the sibling's material table and calls the forward at a reference length to extract the geometry constant. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding L_max back through `computeDriveshaftCritical` (across materials) and confirming its recommended-max speed equals the target RPM. Full wiring: `tools-data` (placed in the later Group K section, NOT the exact-12 audit block beside driveshaft-crit), `tile-meta`, `citations` (Euler-Bernoulli / Spicer, GOVERNANCE.general + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> driveshaft-crit / shaft-torsion / displacement-cr), the `app.js` mechanic declare, the `_simpleRenderer` registration with the material select, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(mechanic): spec-v691 screw conveyor speed for a target capacity (screw-conveyor inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `screw-conveyor-rpm` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the inverse of `screw-conveyor`. The forward tile returns the volumetric capacity from a screw speed; the commissioning question is the reverse: what auger speed (or VFD setting) hits a target throughput. From `Q = flight_area x (pitch/12) x (rpm x 60) x loading`, `rpm = target_ft3_hr / (flight_area x (pitch/12) x 60 x loading)`. The pinned example: a 9 in screw with a 2.5 in shaft, 9 in pitch, at 30% loading needs **40 RPM** for **220 ft^3/hr**; double the target, **80 RPM** (capacity is linear in speed, though CEMA caps the speed by diameter). Divide a mass rate by the bulk density for the volumetric target. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the RPM back through `computeScrewConveyor` and confirming it returns the target capacity. Full wiring: `tools-data` (placed in the later Group K section, NOT the exact-12 audit block beside screw-conveyor), `tile-meta`, `citations` (CEMA Book No. 350, GOVERNANCE.general + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> screw-conveyor / affinity-laws / belt-pulley), the `app.js` mechanic declare, the `_simpleRenderer` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(agriculture): spec-v690 grain bin wall height for a target capacity (grain-bin-capacity inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `grain-bin-height-for-capacity` (Group L, calc-agriculture.js), the inverse of `grain-bin-capacity`. The forward tile returns the bushel capacity from a wall height; the storage-sizing question is the reverse: how tall a wall does a bin of this diameter need to hold the bushels I want. From `bushels = (floor_area x eave + roof_cone) x packing x 0.8036`, `eave = (target_ft3/packing - cone_ft3) / floor_area`. The pinned example: storing 12,875 bu in a 30 ft bin with an 8 ft peaked fill needs a **20 ft** wall; a 42 ft bin needs only **8.9 ft** (capacity grows with the square of the diameter but linearly with the wall). A peaked fill is credited first; if the cone alone meets the target the tile reports it. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the eave back through `computeGrainBin` and confirming it returns the target bushels. ### feat(mechanic): spec-v689 hydraulic flow limit from drive power (hydraulic-pump-horsepower inverse); 2026-07-13

The forward tile returns the drive horsepower from a flow and pressure; the system-matching question is the reverse: how much flow can my power unit deliver at this pressure for the HP it has. From `drive_hp = (gpm x psi / 1714) / efficiency`, `gpm = 1714 x drive_hp x efficiency / psi`. The pinned example: a **13.7 HP** drive at **2,000 psi** and 0.85 efficiency moves **10 GPM**; at **3,000 psi**, **6.7 GPM** (flow trades against pressure at fixed power, the constant-horsepower curve). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the max flow back through `computeHydraulicPumpHorsepower` and confirming it returns the drive HP. ### feat(electrical): spec-v688 point-method required candela for a target (point-illuminance inverse); 2026-07-13

The forward tile returns the illuminance at a point from a fixture's candlepower; the design question is the reverse: what intensity must the fixture aim toward the point to hit the target footcandles. From `E = I x cos^3(angle) / height^2`, `I = E x height^2 / cos^3(angle)` (E in footcandles, lux / 10.764). The pinned example: hitting 10 fc from 10 ft up straight below needs **1,000 cd**; the same 10 fc at 30 deg off-nadir needs **1,540 cd** (the cos^3 penalty). An fc/lux unit select handles either spec. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the required candela back through `computePointIlluminance` and confirming it returns the target footcandles. ### feat(fire): spec-v687 standby battery runtime from capacity (standby-battery-sizing inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `standby-battery-runtime` (Group A, calc-lowvoltage.js), the inverse of `standby-battery-sizing` (NFPA 72 §10.6). The forward tile returns the required battery amp-hours for a standby period; the service question is the reverse: how long will this installed battery hold up the panel. From `required_Ah = (I_standby x Hs + I_alarm x alarm_min/60) x derate`, `Hs = (battery_Ah/derate - alarm_Ah) / I_standby`. The pinned example: a **14.6 Ah** battery at 0.5 A standby, 2 A / 5 min alarm, 1.2 derate holds **24 h**; an **18 Ah** battery, **30 h**. The derate divides the usable capacity, the alarm reserve is subtracted first, and a battery too small to cover even the alarm reserve is rejected. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the standby hours back through `computeStandbyBatterySizing` and confirming it returns the battery capacity. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (NFPA 72 §10.6, GOVERNANCE.fire + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> standby-battery-sizing / battery-runtime / off-grid-battery), the `app.js` lowvoltage declare, the hand-written renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(hvac): spec-v686 fan max airflow from motor power (fan-motor-bhp inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `fan-motor-max-airflow` (Group C, calc-hvac.js), the inverse of `fan-motor-bhp` (spec-v20). The forward tile returns the brake horsepower from an airflow and static; the troubleshooting question is the reverse: how much air can this installed motor move against the system static. From `BHP = (CFM x TSP) / (6356 x fan_efficiency)`, `CFM_max = 6356 x BHP x fan_efficiency / TSP`, and a nameplate (motor) HP converts to brake HP first with the drive efficiency. The pinned example: a **1.94 BHP** fan at 65% against **2 in wc** moves **4,000 CFM**; against **3 in wc**, **2,670 CFM** (airflow falls as static rises). A motor/brake basis select handles the nameplate-vs-shaft distinction. The note is explicit that this is the power ceiling, not a guaranteed delivery (the fan/system curves set the real CFM). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the max CFM back through `computeFanMotorBhp` and confirming it returns the brake HP. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (AMCA/ASHRAE, GOVERNANCE.mechanical + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> fan-motor-bhp / fan-affinity-laws / duct-sizing / cfm-per-ton), the `app.js` hvac declare, the hand-written renderer with the power-basis makeSelect, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### fix(shells): raise the group-shell gzip cap 48 -> 52 KB for Group E growth; 2026-07-13

The v664-v684 inverse-tile campaign added three Group E construction inverses (hoop-stress-mawp v668, thermal-stress-max-deltat v674, helical-pile-torque v681), and the construction group hub lists every tile, so `groups/construction/index.html` grew to ~48.2 KB gzipped and crossed the 48 KB `GROUP_GZIP_CAP` at v681. Because the prerendered-shell-content gate (`check-shells.mjs`) runs only in CI's integration job and not in push-time `npm run lint`, push lint stayed green while CI went red from v681 through v684 (the same CI-only blind spot the v547 comment records). Raised the cap to 52 KB, restoring ~8% headroom; `check-shells` and `check-shell-mobile` both pass. No tile behavior changes.

### feat(fabrication): spec-v683 punch capacity max hole or thickness (punch-force inverse); 2026-07-13

The forward tile returns the punching force for a hole and thickness; the shop-floor question is the reverse: what is the biggest hole (or thickest material) my press can punch. A solve-for select picks which unknown. The pinned example: a **9.8 ton** press punches a **0.5 in** hole in **0.25 in** of 50 ksi-shear steel -- or a half-inch hole in that same quarter-inch plate. ### feat(construction): spec-v681 helical pile acceptance torque for a target capacity (helical-pile inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `helical-pile-torque` (Group E, calc-construction.js), the inverse of `helical-pile` (spec-v7). The forward tile returns the pile capacity from an installation torque; the installer's question is the reverse: what torque must the drive head reach to confirm the design capacity. From `ultimate = Kt x torque` and `allowable = ultimate / FS`, `torque = (allowable x FS) / Kt = ultimate / Kt`. The pinned example: a 22,500 lb allowable at FS 2 on a 1.5 in solid shaft (Kt 10) needs **4,500 ft-lb**; a lower-Kt 3.5 in pipe (Kt 5) needs **9,000 ft-lb**. An allowable/ultimate basis select and the shaft-type select (shared Kt table) match the sibling. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the acceptance torque back through `computeHelicalPile` across shaft types and confirming it returns the allowable capacity. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (ICC-ES AC358, GOVERNANCE.engineer_of_record + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> helical-pile / pile-axial-capacity / footing-area), the `app.js` construction declare, the hand-written renderer with shaft + basis makeSelects, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. (No Group E count bump: the audit is a lower bound.) Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,129 -> 1,130 (version 0.271.0).

### feat(machinist): spec-v680 feed for a target turned finish (turning-surface-finish inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `feed-for-surface-finish` (Group K, calc-shop.js), the inverse of `turning-surface-finish` (spec-v40). The forward tile returns the theoretical finish from feed and nose radius; the programming question is the reverse: how fast can I feed and still hold the print finish. From `Rt = f^2 / (8 x nose_radius)`, `f = sqrt(8 x nose_radius x Rt)`, with an Ra target converted by `Rt = 4 x Ra`. The pinned example: holding **25 microinch Ra** with a 1/32 in nose radius allows **0.005 IPR**; a finer **16 microinch Ra** drops it to **0.004 IPR**. An Ra/Rt basis select lets you enter the finish either way. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the max feed back through `computeTurningSurfaceFinish` and confirming it returns the target Ra. ### feat(mechanic): spec-v679 chamber volume for a target compression ratio (displacement-cr inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `chamber-cc-for-cr` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the inverse of `displacement-cr` (spec-v8). The forward tile returns the static compression ratio from the chamber/gasket/deck/dome volumes; the engine-builder's question is the reverse: what chamber volume (or how much to mill the head / how large a piston dish) hits a target CR. From `CR = (cylinder_cc + TDC_volume) / TDC_volume`, `TDC_volume = cylinder_cc / (CR - 1)` and `chamber = TDC_volume - gasket - deck + dome`. The pinned example: a 4.0 x 3.48 in cylinder (716.7 cc) targeting 10.73:1 needs a **64.0 cc** chamber; a pump-gas 9.0:1 target grows it to **79.9 cc**. A target CR too high for the geometry (chamber would go zero/negative) is rejected. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the chamber back through `computeDisplacementCR` (including a domed piston) and confirming it returns the target CR. Full wiring: `tools-data` (placed in the later Group K section, NOT the exact-12 audit block beside displacement-cr), `tile-meta`, `citations` (SAE engine geometry, GOVERNANCE.general + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> displacement-cr / volumetric-efficiency / hp-from-torque), the `app.js` mechanic declare, the `_simpleRenderer` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(stage): spec-v673 distance for a target SPL (spl-distance inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `spl-distance-for-level` (Group N, calc-stage.js), the inverse of `spl-distance`. The forward tile returns the sound pressure level at a given distance; the layout and hearing-safety question is the reverse: at what distance does the level fall to a target (the 85 dB safe line, a noise-spill limit, a monitor position). From `L2 = L1 - 20 log10(d2/d1) + mode_factor + 10 log10(N)`, `d2 = d1 x 10^((L1 + mode_factor + 10 log10(N) - L2) / 20)`. The pinned example: a 110 dB source at 1 ft falls to 84 dB at about **20 ft** free-field, **28 ft** with hemispherical reinforcement. A target at or above the adjusted reference level is rejected (it would sit closer than the reference). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding d2 back through `computeSPL` across all three modes and source counts and confirming the level equals the target. Full wiring: `tools-data` (placed in the later Group N section, NOT the exact-8 audit block beside spl-distance), `tile-meta`, `citations` (inverse-square / ISO 9613-2, GOVERNANCE.rigging + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> spl-distance / spl-atmospheric / time-alignment), the `app.js` stage declare, the `_r` registration with the mode select, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(machining): spec-v672 max bearing load for a target L10 life (bearing-l10-life inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `bearing-max-load` (Group K, calc-machining.js), the inverse of `bearing-l10-life` (spec-v504). The forward tile returns the L10 rating life for a given load; the design question is the reverse: how hard can I load this bearing and still get the life I need. From `L10 = (C/P)^p x 10^6 rev` with `L10 = target_hr x 60 x rpm`, `P_max = C x (10^6 / L10_rev)^(1/p)` (p = 3 ball, 10/3 roller). The pinned example: a ball bearing rated C = 5,000 lbf at 1,750 rpm can carry **1,000 lbf** for a **1,190 hr** L10; a roller bearing carries **1,175 lbf** (gentler 10/3 exponent). Because life scales as the cube of the load ratio, the load limit is only weakly sensitive to the target life. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding P_max back through `computeBearingL10Life` and confirming it returns the target life (both bearing types). Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (ISO 281 / ABMA, GOVERNANCE.general + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> bearing-l10-life / vbelt-drive / shrink-fit / press-fit-pressure), the `app.js` machining declare, the hand-written renderer with the ball/roller makeSelect toggle (the select feeds the compute, satisfying check-dead-inputs), the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(rigging): spec-v670 spanned cable minimum sag for a tension limit (spanline-sag-tension inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `spanline-sag-for-tension` (Group Z, calc-rigging.js), the inverse of `spanline-sag-tension` (spec-v484). The forward tile returns the tension a spanned cable develops from its sag; the safety question is the reverse: how much sag do I need to keep the tension under the rope's working load limit. Solving the support-tension relation `T = w L^2 / (8 d) sqrt(1 + (4 d / L)^2)` for the sag (squaring collapses the (4d/L)^2 term to a constant) gives `d_min = w L^2 / (8 sqrt(T_allow^2 - (w L / 2)^2))`. Crucially this solves the SUPPORT (anchor) tension -- the true maximum in the cable and what the WLL limits -- so it never under-sags, unlike a naive horizontal-tension inverse; the allowable must exceed the w L / 2 vertical reaction or no sag can carry the load. The pinned example: a 100 ft span at 1 lb/ft held to a **502 lb** limit needs at least **2.5 ft** of sag; a **2,500 lb** limit lets you pull to **0.5 ft**. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding d_min back through `computeSpanlineSagTension` and confirming the support tension equals the allowable. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (shallow-cable parabola / ASME B30.9, GOVERNANCE.rigging + 4 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> spanline-sag-tension / wire-rope-strength / sling-angle / block-redirect-load), the `app.js` rigging declare, the hand-written renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. (Group Z has no audit-count or governance test, so no count bump.) Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,118 -> 1,119 (version 0.260.0).

### feat(hvac): spec-v669 chimney height for a target draft (chimney-draft inverse); 2026-07-13

The forward tile returns the natural draft for a given stack height; the design question is the reverse: how tall a stack do I need to produce the net draft the appliance requires. Solving `D_net = net_factor x 0.52 x B x H x (1/T_o - 1/T_m)` for H gives `H = D_net_target / (net_factor x 0.52 x B x (1/T_o - 1/T_m))` (temperatures in Rankine). The pinned example: a 400 F mean flue over 60 F ambient at sea level needs about **30 ft** for a **0.10 in wc** net draft; at 5,000 ft (~12.2 psia) the thinner air demands **36 ft** for the same draft. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the required height back through `computeChimneyDraft` and confirming it returns the target net draft. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (ASHRAE / NFPA 211, GOVERNANCE.general + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> chimney-draft / combustion-air / excess-air-o2 / flue-gas-combustion-eff), the `app.js` hvacservice declare, the hand-written renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(construction): spec-v668 thin-wall vessel max allowable working pressure (hoop-stress-thin-wall inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `hoop-stress-mawp` (Group E, calc-construction.js), the inverse of `hoop-stress-thin-wall` (spec-v361). The forward tile returns the hoop stress `sigma_h = P D / (2 t)` and a demand/capacity ratio for a chosen pressure; the everyday pressure-vessel question is the reverse: what is the maximum allowable working pressure a given wall and diameter can hold at the allowable stress. Solving for P at sigma_h = S_allow gives `P_max = 2 t S_allow / D` (hoop-governed; the longitudinal limit is double). The pinned example: a **12 in** vessel with a **0.25 in** wall at **15,000 psi** allowable holds **625 psi** (longitudinal limit 1,250 psi -- the hoop governs, which is why a cylinder splits lengthwise). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding `P_max` back through `computeHoopStressThinWall` and confirming the hoop stress lands at S_allow (DCR exactly 1). Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (Barlow / thin-wall, GOVERNANCE.general + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases (the bare "mawp" term already belongs to pipe-pressure-rating, so this uses "vessel mawp"), related-tiles (<-> hoop-stress-thin-wall / pipe-pressure-rating / tank-volume / combined-stress-axial-bending), the `app.js` construction declare, the `_simpleRenderer` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(stage): spec-v667 LED tape max run before the far end dims (led-tape-run inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `led-tape-max-run` (Group N, calc-stage.js), the inverse of `led-tape-run` (spec-v543). The forward tile returns the load, PSU size, and end-of-run voltage for a given run length; the design question is the reverse: how far can I run this tape before the far end dims past my tolerance. From the tile's own uniform-load model `drop_pct = power_per_ft x resistance_per_ft x length^2 / (2 x voltage^2) x 100`, setting `drop_pct = tolerance` gives `len_max = voltage x sqrt(2 x (tolerance/100) / (power_per_ft x resistance_per_ft))`. The pinned example: a **4.4 W/ft**, **12 V** strip at **0.05 ohm/ft** walls out at about **11.4 ft** for a 10% drop; a **24 V** strip reaches about **22.9 ft** (double -- the run scales with the voltage). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding `len_max` back through `computeLedTapeRun` and confirming the drop equals the tolerance (asserting the drop value, not the too-long flag, which sits on the float boundary at the max run). Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (GOVERNANCE.general + 2 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases (distinct from the forward tile's), related-tiles (<-> led-tape-run / voltage-drop / power-distro / led-video-wall), the `app.js` stage declare, the `_r` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. ### feat(electrical): spec-v666 battery room max charge current from available airflow (battery-hydrogen-vent inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `battery-vent-max-current` (Group A, calc-electrical.js), the inverse of `battery-hydrogen-vent` (spec-v518). The forward tile runs IEEE 1635 forward -- exhaust airflow `Q = 0.054 x I x N` cfm from the cell count and charge current; the other real case is the reverse: the fan is installed and moves a known airflow, so how much charge current can the room safely support. That is `I_max = Q / (0.054 x N)`. It carries the same cells-not-jars trap: N counts individual 2 V cells, so twenty-four 12 V jars is 144 cells, not 24. The pinned example: a **100 cfm** exhaust over a **24-cell** string supports **77 A**; counted as 144 cells it is only **12.9 A** (one-sixth, the same six-fold factor the forward tile flags). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding `I_max` back through `computeBatteryHydrogenVent` and confirming it returns the input airflow. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (IEEE 1635, GOVERNANCE.general + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 5 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> battery-hydrogen-vent / standby-battery-sizing / off-grid-battery / battery-runtime), the `app.js` electrical declare, the hand-written renderer, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. The forward tile runs NEC 352.44 forward -- movement `delta_L` from a run length and temperature swing, against the quarter-inch expansion-fitting trigger; the field question is the reverse: how long a straight PVC run can I make before I must add a fitting. That is `L_max = trigger / (coefficient x 12 x |delta_T|)`. Bundled PVC coefficient 3.38e-5 in/in/deg-F and the 0.25 in trigger are the sibling's editable defaults. The pinned example: at a **50 deg F** swing a run over about **12.3 ft** reaches the trigger; at **100 deg F** it is about **6.2 ft** (the run varies inversely with the swing). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding `L_max` back through `computeConduitThermalExpansion` and confirming the movement equals the trigger. ### feat(stage): spec-v664 absorption needed for a target RT60 (room-acoustics inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `room-absorption-target` (Group N, calc-stage.js), the inverse of `room-acoustics` (spec-v120). The forward tile runs the Sabine equation forward -- RT60 from a room's volume and total absorption; the everyday design question is the reverse: given a *target* RT60, how much absorption must the room have, and how much treatment do I add over what the bare room already provides. That is `A_required = 0.049 x V / RT60_target` sabins, with the extra to install being `max(0, A_required - A_existing)`. Public-domain Sabine physics; the 0.049 imperial coefficient is already in the sibling and stays an editable field. The pinned example: a **5,000 ft^3** room targeting **0.6 s** needs **408 sabins**; with **250 sabins** already present, add **158 sabins** of treatment (about 200 ft^2 of a panel at coefficient 0.8). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding `A_required` back through `computeRoomAcoustics` and confirming it reproduces the target RT60, and pins the already-met floor at zero. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (GOVERNANCE.general + editionNote + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 6 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> room-acoustics / spl-distance / decibel-converter / spl-atmospheric), the `app.js` stage declare, the `_r` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings, and the fuzzer block. (No Group N audit-count edit: this section is outside the parsed audit block.) Sizes the absorption, not the axial modes (geometry sets those). Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,112 -> 1,113 (version 0.254.0).

### feat(hvac): spec-v663 dry-bulb from enthalpy and humidity ratio (moist-air-enthalpy inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `drybulb-from-enthalpy` (Group C, calc-hvac.js), the inverse of `moist-air-enthalpy` (spec-v375). The forward tile computes enthalpy from dry-bulb and W; a psychrometric analysis often gives the enthalpy and W (e.g. a coil leaving state) but not the dry-bulb, and recovering it is the reverse. Solving h = 0.240 t + W (1061 + 0.444 t) for t gives t = (h - 1061 W) / (0.240 + 0.444 W). First-principles; the ASHRAE coefficients (0.240, 1061, 0.444) are already in the sibling. The pinned example: 31.48 Btu/lb at W 0.0112 recovers an **80 F** dry-bulb; dry air (W 0) at 19.2 Btu/lb is 80 F (t = h/0.240). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the recovered dry-bulb back through `computeMoistAirEnthalpy` at the same W and confirming it returns the input enthalpy. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (GOVERNANCE.general + editionNote + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 6 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> moist-air-enthalpy / cooling-coil-total-load / shr-latent), the `app.js` hvac declare, the `_rEnv` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings + derivations, and the fuzzer block. (No Group C audit-count edit: that test uses a lower-bound count.) Single-direction (dry-bulb from h + W); the W-from-(h, t) direction is a natural future extension. Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,111 -> 1,112 (version 0.253.0).

### feat(mechanic): spec-v662 horsepower from quarter-mile ET (trap-speed ET companion); 2026-07-13

Lands `et-horsepower` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the ET companion of `trap-speed-horsepower` (spec-v325). The forward tile estimates power from trap speed and reports ET as a companion output but cannot take ET as an input; many timeslips give ET more prominently. Inverting Hale's ET relation ET = 5.825 x (weight/HP)^(1/3) gives HP = weight x (5.825/ET)^3. First-principles; the 5.825 constant is already in the sibling. The pinned example: a 3,200 lb car running a 12.63 s quarter made **~314 hp**; a quicker 11.5 s ET implies ~416 hp (a ~9% ET drop is a ~32% power gain, the cube law). The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the ET the trap-speed tile computes for a 108 mph trap back through this tile and recovering the same horsepower. Full wiring: `tools-data`, `tile-meta`, `citations` (GOVERNANCE.general + editionNote + 3 assumptions), compute-map, 2 machine-verified worked examples, 6 collision-checked aliases, related-tiles (<-> trap-speed-horsepower / hp-from-torque / dyno-correction), the `app.js` mechanic declare, the `_simpleRenderer` registration, the `// dims:` annotation, regenerated corpus + tile-index + citation-strings + derivations, and the fuzzer block. Lazy-loaded, absent from home first paint. Catalog 1,110 -> 1,111 (version 0.252.0).

### feat(mechanic): spec-v661 injector max horsepower capacity (injector-size inverse); 2026-07-13

Lands `injector-max-hp` (Group K, calc-mechanic.js), the inverse of `injector-size` (spec-v323). The forward tile sizes injectors for a target power; this answers what a tuner with injectors in hand asks -- "how much power will these support?" Inverting inj_lbh = HP x BSFC / (n_cyl x duty) gives HP_max = inj_lbh x n_cyl x duty / BSFC. First-principles; the constants (10.5 cc/min conversion, default BSFC 0.50, default duty 0.80) are already in the sibling. The pinned example: eight 31.25 lb/h injectors at 80% duty and BSFC 0.50 support **400 hp**; the same injectors on boost (BSFC 0.60) fall to 333 hp -- a richer tune caps power. The bounds-fuzzer proves the exact inverse by feeding the max HP back through `computeInjectorSize` to recover the 31.25 lb/h, and pins the BSFC scaling and the cc/min conversion (328.125 cc/min = 31.25 lb/h -> the same 400 hp).
